coredata.dc:description: Waterborne coating is eco-friendly, but exhibits limited anti-corrosion performance. Graphene is an ultrahigh anti-corrosion material for its perfect impermeability. Unfortunately, graphene tends to aggregate and randomly align in waterborne resin to form conductive network to accelerate corrosion. Herein, N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) modified graphene (N-CQDs@Gr) was prepared via π-π interactions between N-CQDs and graphene, stably dispersing in water over 90 days and showing good dispersibility and compatibility in waterborne resin. Meanwhile, we designed a spin coating method and successfully fabricated orientated N-CQDs@Gr/waterborne epoxy (N-CQDs@Gr/WEP) coating, which exhibited long-term anti-corrosion performance. The impedance modulus of N-CQDs@Gr/WEP coating was about 200 times higher than that of waterborne epoxy (WEP) coating after 90 days of immersion in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution and still remained above 109 ohm cm2 after 260 days of immersion. The coating also exhibited outstanding corrosion resistance under harsh oxygen environment. After 10 days of immersion in harsh oxygen environment, the coating still protected the metal well, and the impedance modulus of the coating was still maintained above 1010 ohm cm2 which was about three orders of magnitude higher than that of WEP coating. The excellent anti-corrosion performance of N-CQDs@Gr/WEP coating was interpreted as the reinforced interface bonding force, the orientation of graphene and the protective film covering metal surface. The orientation of graphene maximized the barrier effect of graphene and avoided the formation of graphene conductive network. The protective film formed in normal environment was mainly N-CQD@Fe3+ complex film, whereas in harsh oxygen environment was mainly passive film.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Waterborne coating is eco-friendly, but exhibits limited anti-corrosion performance. Graphene is an ultrahigh anti-corrosion material for its perfect impermeability. Unfortunately, graphene tends to aggregate and randomly align in waterborne resin to form conductive network to accelerate corrosion. Herein, N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) modified graphene (N-CQDs@Gr) was prepared via π-π interactions between N-CQDs and graphene, stably dispersing in water over 90 days and showing good dispersibility and compatibility in waterborne resin. Meanwhile, we designed a spin coating method and successfully fabricated orientated N-CQDs@Gr/waterborne epoxy (N-CQDs@Gr/WEP) coating, which exhibited long-term anti-corrosion performance. The impedance modulus of N-CQDs@Gr/WEP coating was about 200 times higher than that of waterborne epoxy (WEP) coating after 90 days of immersion in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution and still remained above 109 ohm cm2 after 260 days of immersion. The coating also exhibited outstanding corrosion resistance under harsh oxygen environment. After 10 days of immersion in harsh oxygen environment, the coating still protected the metal well, and the impedance modulus of the coating was still maintained above 1010 ohm cm2 which was about three orders of magnitude higher than that of WEP coating. The excellent anti-corrosion performance of N-CQDs@Gr/WEP coating was interpreted as the reinforced interface bonding force, the orientation of graphene and the protective film covering metal surface. The orientation of graphene maximized the barrier effect of graphene and avoided the formation of graphene conductive network. The protective film formed in normal environment was mainly N-CQD@Fe3+ complex film, whereas in harsh oxygen environment was mainly passive film.

ce:section.ce:para: Metal equipment generally suffers from serious corrosion in harsh environments, especially in high-pressure air injection oil recovery, large compressed air energy storage and other high-pressure oxidation environments. The corrosion in these environments is mainly caused by high-pressure oxygen, in which oxygen could directly participate in the cathodic reduction reaction to increase the cathodic reaction rate and almost all of the common corrosion inhibitors cannot work effectively [1,2]. Additionally, the presence of chloride ions is not negligible due to the fact that the strong synergistic effect between chloride ions and oxygen would further aggravate the corrosion [3,4]. The corrosion rate in these harsh environments was increased by more than two orders of magnitude, greatly accelerating the deterioration of metal equipment [1,3,5]. This severely limited the human exploration of nature. Thus, it is urgent to develop an effective high-performance anti-corrosion and oxygen resistant strategy to suppress the severe corrosion in harsh oxygen environments.

Anti-corrosion coatings, especially waterborne anti-corrosion coatings containing less toxic heavy metals and Volatile organic compounds (VOCs), have been widely applied in industry [6,7]. Unfortunately, owing to the presence of hydrophilic groups in waterborne resin, the anti-corrosion performance of conventional waterborne coatings is unsatisfactory, especially when exposed to harsh corrosive environments [8]. Graphene shows perfect impermeability to any atoms or molecules, making it a very promising candidate for the ultrahigh anti-corrosion material [9,10]. However, since the strong van der Waals forces and π-π interactions between graphene nanosheets, and the critical wetting surface tension of graphene is lower than the surface tension of water, it is difficult for waterborne resin to achieve high infiltration and dispersion of graphene [6,11]. Besides, the agglomerated and randomly aligned graphene nanosheets in waterborne coating tend to connect with each other and the steel plate to form graphene conductive network and trigger microgalvanic corrosion of metal once the corrosive media penetrate [8,12]. To enhance the anti-corrosion performance of graphene waterborne coating, two necessary conditions must be fulfilled, (1) improving the dispersibility and compatibility of graphene in waterborne coating and (2) restraining the formation of graphene conductive network.

In general, chemical modification and noncovalent functionalization of graphene have been applied to achieve the first condition. Compared with chemical modification, noncovalent functionalization, such as π-π stacking, is highly efficient, ease of control, and can retain the original structure of graphene to the utmost extent [6,13]. Aromatic compounds and surfactants are the most commonly employed. However, they are usually expensive and difficult to synthesize and have limited ability to disperse graphene [14]. Therefore, finding a low-cost and high-efficiency material to modify graphene and matching graphene with the appropriate corresponding waterborne resin to improve the dispersibility and compatibility of graphene in waterborne coating are highly desirable, but are still challenges.

In regard to the second condition, graphene orientation that graphene nanosheets are arranged parallel to each other and metal substrate, is a direct way to satisfy this condition. At the same time, Cui et al. [6] also deemed that the orientation of graphene would significantly improve the anti-corrosion capability of coatings, but was rarely achieved, which was extremely difficult. Notably, high centrifugal and shear forces may enable well-dispersed graphene nanosheets to be aligned parallel to the metal surface [15,16]. Hence, preparing well-dispersed graphene nanosheets and designing a facile method to orientate graphene in waterborne coating are essential for this work.

Recently, N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) with high water solubility, simple synthesis process and low manufacturing cost, have gained ever-growing concern [17,18]. It is reported that adding N-CQDs into polymer could not only fill the defects caused by solvent evaporation during the curing process of polymer, but also increase the crosslink density of the polymer system [19,20]. Atchudan et al. [17] and Song et al. [18] demonstrated that the amine, hydroxyl and carboxylic groups on the surface of N-CQDs could coordinate with Fe3+ ions to form stable complex. Researchers also reported that N-CQDs possessed charged sites, which were favorable for O2 adsorption and O-O bonds weaking [21,22]. These might be beneficial for improving the anti-corrosion performance of coatings. Most important of all, N-CQDs possess π-π conjugated network and rich functional groups [23], which are expected to facilitate the dispersion of graphene in aqueous solution via π-π interactions between graphene and N-CQDs.

In this work, N-CQDs were used as stabilizer to successfully disperse graphene (N-CQDs@Gr) in aqueous solution for more than 90 days. We also designed a facile spin coating method and creatively fabricated orientated N-CQDs@Gr/waterborne epoxy (N-CQDs@Gr/WEP) coating with long-term anti-corrosion performance. Amazing, the steel plate still well protected and the impedance modulus of the coating still maintained at 1010 ohm cm2 after 10 days of immersion in harsh oxygen corrosive environment. The enhanced anti-corrosion performance was attributed to the synergistic effect of orientated graphene and N-CQDs. The former maximized the barrier effect of graphene and avoided the formation of conductive network. The latter (1) improved the dispersibility and compatibility of graphene in waterborne epoxy matrix, (2) strengthened the interface bonding force between filler and waterborne epoxy matrix, and (3) formed protective film at the coating/steel plate interface. This work greatly promotes the practical application of graphene, opens a new development direction for waterborne coating in harsh corrosive environments, and also shows bright promise in numerous fields requiring barrier against moisture, oxygen and other gases and liquids.
Please refer to Supplementary material for the detail information.

N-CQDs were prepared by a hydrothermal method [24]. First, citric acid monohydrate (21.02 g) and ethylenediamine (6700 μL) were dissolved in 200 mL of distilled water and stirred for 40 min to obtain clear solution. Second, the solution was transferred to Teflon lined autoclaves and heated at 200 °C for 4.5 h. After the reaction, the autoclaves were cooled to room temperature naturally. Third, the brown-black and transparent product was filtered through a 0.1 μm nylon membrane and further purified with deionized water in dialysis bags (500 Da) for 36 h. Final, the dispersion was lyophilized in refrigerant drier (FD-1A-50, Shanghai Yuming Instrument Co. Ltd.) to obtain N-CQDs powder. The N-CQDs synthesized by this method were environment-friendly and low-cost, and the yield could reach gram scale.

Typically, 0.25 g of graphene was added to 100 mL of N-CQDs aqueous dispersion (1 mg/mL), followed by ultrasonication for 3 h and stirring for 24 h at room temperature. Then, the dispersion was filtrated through a 0.1 μm nylon membrane under continuous stirring. It is important to note that the filtrate (N-CQDs aqueous dispersion) could be reused again after freeze-drying, further reducing the manufacturing cost. Final, the N-CQDs@Gr powder was obtained after lyophilizing the N-CQDs@Gr dispersion in refrigerant drier.

The general procedures for fabricating N-CQDs@Gr/WEP coating were as follows (Fig. 1 c). First, the N-CQDs@Gr powder (1 wt% of the total mass of epoxy resin and waterborne curing agent) was dispersed in distilled water, followed by ultrasonication for 30 min. Second, 1.5 g waterborne curing agent was added to the dispersion, and then was ultrasonicated for 30 min and stirred for 30 min. Third, epoxy resin was added (1 g) and stirred with the above mixture for 20 min. Final, the mixture was evenly distributed onto the steel plate surface and rotated by a spin coater subsequently (KW-4A spin coater, the Institute of Microelectronics, Chinese academy of sciences, China) at a speed of 2000 rpm for 30 s. The prepared coating was pre-cured at 60 °C for 2 h, whereupon the above steps were repeated four times and the final sample was cured at 60 °C for 3 h and 90 °C for 3 h. Besides, the waterborne epoxy coating and waterborne epoxy composite coatings with 1 wt% graphene and 1 wt% N-CQDs were also fabricated according to the above steps for comparison and named as WEP, Gr/WEP and N-CQDs/WEP coatings, respectively. The final dry thicknesses of all the coatings were 95 ± 5 μm.
Please refer to Supplementary material for the specific information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and characterization of N-CQDs and N-CQDs@Gr
ce:section.ce:para: The formation process of N-CQDs was conducted by first condensing citric acid and ethylenediamine to form polymer-like N-CQDs, whereupon they were carbonized to form N-CQDs (Fig. 1a), which were about 3–7 nm and exhibited excellent fluorescence performance under a hand-held UV lamp (Fig. 2 a–c). FT-IR and XPS confirmed that the surface of N-CQDs contained high oxygen-containing and nitrogen-containing functional groups such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, carbonyl, amino, amide and epoxy groups (Fig. 3 a–d, Fig. S2a) [18,25,26]. After ionizing in water, these negatively charged groups endowed N-CQDs with strong electrostatic repulsion to overcome the van der Waals forces and hydrophobic forces. As a consequence, the N-CQDs generated a brown and transparent dispersion (1.25 mg/mL) and exhibited excellent dispersibility after a simple sonication in water, and an obvious Tyndall effect could be observed when a laser beam passed through (Fig. 2d).

By contrast, although prolonging the ultrasonication time of graphene aqueous dispersion (1.25 mg/mL) to 3 h could hardly disperse the graphene nanosheets well in water and obvious precipitation and floatage could be observed (Fig. 2d). This was due to the fact that there were almost no functional groups on the surface of graphene nanosheets so that not enough surface charge could be generated to overcome the van der Waals forces and hydrophobic forces (Fig. S2b) [27]. The fabrication process of N-CQDs@Gr was shown in Fig. 1b, in which the inherent impermeability of graphene was not destroy. The TEM image of N-CQDs@Gr vividly showed that the N-CQDs successfully adsorbed on the surface of graphene (Fig. 2e). The presences of N–H in FT-IR spectrum and N element in XPS spectrum of N-CQDs@Gr further proved this (Fig. 3b, Fig. S2b).

Amazingly, the N-CQDs@Gr aqueous dispersion (1.25 mg/mL) also exhibited obvious Tyndall effect, and still maintained homogeneous dispersion without obvious macroscopic agglomeration after 90 days of storage and could retain black over ten months (Fig. 2d, Fig. S3). This was originated from the absorbed N-CQDs on the surface of graphene, which endowed graphene with rich surface functional groups to solve the problem of surface energy mismatch between graphene and water [27]. More surprisingly, the N-CQDs@Gr dispersion could stably disperse in strong acidic (pH = 4, hydrochloric acid solution) and alkaline (pH = 12, sodium hydroxide solution) solution for more than 10 days (Fig. S4). Meanwhile, the precipitates in strong acidic and alkaline solution could be stably dispersed in aqueous solution again after being filtered and washed. Additionally, after the stable N-CQDs@Gr dispersion being centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 20 min, the underlying N-CQDs@Gr slurry could still stably disperse in water after drying. The above results indicated that N-CQDs could strongly adsorbed on the surface of graphene even after high-speed centrifugation and immersion in strong acidic and alkaline solution. If commercialized, this environment-friendly and low-cost water-redispersible graphene powder is expected to largely facilitate the transportation.

UV-vis and XPS spectra were employed to explore the π-π interactions between N-CQDs and graphene nanosheets. UV-vis spectra of N-CQDs, graphene and N-CQDs@Gr were shown in Fig. 3e. Two characteristic absorption peaks mainly concentrated on ∼ 340 nm and ∼ 240 nm appearing in N-CQDs, which were associated with the n-π* transition corresponding to the N-rich amine functional groups on the surface and the π-π* transition aromatic CC bonds, respectively [28]. The graphene presented a peak at 278 nm, belonging to the π-π* transition of C = C. For the N-CQDs@Gr, the peak for π-π* was blue shifted to 268 nm, which provided direct evidence for the π-π interactions between N-CQDs and graphene [29]. The C1s XPS spectrum of N-CQDs@Gr was shown in Fig. 3f. Notably, the peak centered at 290.2 eV was attributed to the bond of the π-π* shake-up between N-CQDs and graphene [14], further confirming that the N-CQDs were successfully adsorbed on the surface of graphene via π-π interactions between N-CQDs and graphene.

Interestingly, the N-CQDs@Gr dispersion could be fabricated into N-CQDs@Gr film via a simple routine filtration and drying (overnight at 80 °C) processes, which could not be achieved using graphene dispersion (Fig. S5a–c). This might be due to the fact that the existence of amino, hydroxyl, carboxyl and epoxy groups on the surface of N-CQDs endowed the adjacent graphene nanosheets with hydrogen bonds, van der Waals bonds and covalent bonds, acting as a link to connect adjacent graphene nanosheets. It is worth noting that although the N-CQDs@Gr powder was water-dispersible, the N-CQDs@Gr film exhibited good stability in water and showed no obvious damage even after vigorous shaking in water (Fig. S5d). Of particular significance is that, the electric conductivity of the N-CQDs@Gr film is measured to be 2668 S/m, indicating that the N-CQDs showed no obvious adverse effect on the conductivity of graphene compared with the insulating polymers or surfactants commonly used to disperse graphene [30]. This further expanded the application fields of N-CQDs@Gr, such as in supercapacitor and electronic product fields. Meanwhile, it also demonstrated that it is necessary to orientate graphene in coating to suppress the formation of conductive network.

The dispersibility and compatibility of fillers as well as the orientation of sheets in waterborne epoxy matrix affected the anti-corrosion performance of the coatings strongly [14,29,31], which were analyzed via SEM observation of the cross-section for the coatings. It is demonstrated that the fracture surface of WEP coating was comparatively smooth, but there were still some holes (red circles) (Fig. 4 a1, a2), which may be because the cross-linking between epoxy resin and curing agent was not good enough. After adding graphene nanosheets into waterborne epoxy matrix (Fig. 4b1, b2), the graphene nanosheets agglomerated seriously and clear gaps could be observed between graphene nanosheets and waterborne epoxy matrix (represented by yellow dotted circles) due to the poor dispersibility and compatibility of graphene in waterborne epoxy matrix.

The hydroxyl, amino, carboxyl and epoxy groups on the surface of N-CQDs could form hydrogen bonds and covalent bonds with the waterborne epoxy matrix and adjacent N-CQDs [19] (Fig. 5 a). Moreover, since N-CQDs possessed aromatic heterocyclic structure, there were Van der Waals bonds between adjacent N-CQDs [19]. After adding N-CQDs into waterborne epoxy matrix, the fracture surface of N-CQDs/WEP coating was smoother than that of WEP coating, and there were almost no defects (Fig. 4c1, c2). This demonstrated that the bond interactions between adjacent N-CQDs, and between N-CQDs and waterborne epoxy matrix made the waterborne epoxy network denser [20]. The glass transition region (Tg) values increased from 83.7 °C to 87.3 °C from WEP coating to N-CQDs/WEP coating (Fig. S6a), further confirming that the addition of N-CQDs enhanced the cross-linking density of N-CQDs/WEP coating [32].

As expected, the graphene nanosheets were relatively uniformly distributed in N-CQDs@Gr/WEP coating without agglomeration and the gaps between graphene nanosheets and waterborne epoxy matrix have almost disappeared (Fig. 4d1, d2). Furthermore, the Tg values increased from 87.8 °C to 90.3 °C from Gr/WEP coating to N-CQDs@Gr/WEP coating [32] (Fig. S6b). These indicated that the modification of N-CQDs prominently improved the dispersibility and compatibility of graphene in waterborne epoxy matrix and strengthened the interface bonding force between graphene and waterborne epoxy matrix [33]. In addition, the graphene nanosheets were nearly perfectly orientated in horizontal direction in N-CQDs@Gr/WEP coating (Fig. 4d2), which was not achieved in Gr/WEP coating (Fig. 4b2). This manifested that improving the dispersibility and compatibility of graphene in waterborne epoxy matrix was important for the orientation of graphene. The mechanism for the orientation of graphene was that the centrifugal and shear forces generated during the high-speed rotation process gave 2D nanosheets an orientation that paralleling to the steel plate surface [15,16,34,35]. Moreover, the enhanced interface bonding force between graphene and waterborne epoxy matrix and the increasing packing order degree of graphene gave rise to the decrease in N-CQDs@Gr/WEP coating’s porosity [29,36]. In brief, (1) the improved dispersibility and compatibility of graphene and the reinforcement of filler/waterborne epoxy matrix interface reducing the defects in N-CQDs@Gr/WEP coating, and (2) the orientation of graphene maximizing the corrosion media barrier effect of graphene and inhibiting the direct connections of graphene-graphene and graphene-metal, are of considerable importance to the outstanding anti-corrosion performance of N-CQDs@Gr/WEP coating [31,37].

The orientation of graphene nanosheets in waterborne epoxy matrix was also evidenced by X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Fig. 5b). Shioya et al. [38] demonstrated that the diffraction peak intensity was proportional to the concentration of (002) planes of graphite satisfying the Bragg’s equation around 2θ = 26.4°. When the graphene nanosheets are randomly aligned, there will few (002) planes of graphite can satisfy the Bragg’s equation, resulting in a much weaker diffraction peak. Nevertheless, when the graphene nanosheets are orientated perpendicular to the diffraction direction, almost all the (002) planes of graphite can generate diffraction peak around 2θ = 26.4°, so that the diffraction intensity will be observably enhanced. As shown in Fig. 5b, the N-CQDs@Gr/WEP coating showed a much sharper diffraction peak around 2θ = 26.4° than that of Gr/WEP coating, which further confirmed that the graphene nanosheets were successfully orientated in the N-CQDs@Gr/WEP coating.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Corrosion behaviors of scratched coatings in normal environment

ce:section.ce:para: Many researchers have reported that the rich and diverse functional groups (amine, hydroxyl and carboxylic) on the surface of N-doped carbon quantum dots could coordinated with Fe3+ ions to form stable N-CQDs@Fe3+ complex (Fig. 7a) [17,18]. As shown in Fig. S7a, b, the fluorescence of N-CQDs was quenched after adding Fe3+ ions solution (prepared by dissolving iron (Ш) chloride hexahydrate in distilled water) to N-CQDs dispersion due to the charge/electron transfer between N-CQDs and Fe3+ ions [18,39]. Compared with the FT-IR spectrum of N-CQDs dispersion, an evident change was observed after adding Fe3+ ions solution (Fig. S8), further manifesting that Fe3+ ions could coordinate with the functional groups on the surface of N-CQDs [39,40]. These results proved that the N-CQDs synthesized in this work could coordinate with Fe3+ ions to form stable complex. This made N-CQDs a possible steel plate corrosion inhibitor.

To verify the corrosion inhibition properties of N-CQDs, the potentiodynamic polarization curves of Q235 steel plates after 24 h of immersion in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution without and with 200 mg/L of N-CQDs were recorded (Fig. 6
a). The relevant corrosive parameters, including corrosion potential (Ecorr ), corrosion current density (icorr ), anodic and cathodic Tafel slopes (βa and βc ), the degree of surface overage (θ) as well as the corrosion inhibition efficiency (η) were summarized in Table S1. The θ and η values were calculated from the icorr values with the following equations [41]:

As shown in Fig. 6a, both cathodic and anodic region were transferred to lower current density after adding N-CQDs, indicating that the addition of N-CQDs had an effect on the cathodic and anodic reactions. After introducing N-CQDs, the icorr reduced from 7.022 × 10−5 A cm−2 to 1.351 × 10−5 A cm−2, and the values of η was about 80.8 %. Furthermore, the Ecorr moved positively from −0.858 V to −0.816 V, which was less than 85 mV. The result revealed that N-CQDs acted as a mixed-type inhibitor which could inhibit both the anodic (steel plate dissolution) and cathodic reaction (oxygen reduction) [42,43]. This was due to the fact that the N-CQDs could adsorb on the surface of the steel plate to form a powerful protective film mainly through interactions between the heteroatoms with free electrons (N and O) in N-CQDs and the unoccupied 3d orbital of the Fe3+ ions, so that limited the mass transfer of corrosive media (H2O, O2 and Cl−) to the cathodic sites of the steel plate and reduced the corrosion rate of the steel plate [44]. Cui et al. [45] also synthesized similar carbon dots in a different way and proved this. In addition, after 24 h of immersion in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution without N-CQDs, the surface of the steel plate was gravely corroded and produced a large amount of corrosion product (Fig. S9a1, a2). However, the steel plate immersed in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution with 200 mg/L of N-CQDs was protected well (Fig. S9b1), and N element derived from N-CQDs was detected, further affirming the existence of N-CQDs@Fe3+ protective film on the steel plate surface (Fig. S9b2–b6, Fig. S10) [46]. Therefore, the N-CQDs synthesized in this work could be deemed to be an effective inhibitor to the steel plate corrosion in a neutral sodium chloride medium.

The corrosion inhibition behavior of the steel plates in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution without and with 200 mg/L of N-CQDs was also studied using EIS technique (Fig. S11) The EIS data were fitted by ZSimpWin software using the electrical equivalent circuits in Fig. S12a, b. The circuits are composed of solution resistance (Rs), film resistance (Rf), charge-transfer resistance (Rct), and Warburg impedance (Zw). Qf and Qdl are constant phase elements substituted for the film capacitance and double layer capacitance to correct the deviation of the ideal capacitance [45]. The fitting results were listed in Tables S2-S3.

As evidenced from Fig. S11, the impedance moduli at 0.01 Hz (|Z| 0.01 Hz), the breakpoint frequency (fb) and the capacitive loops of the steel plate immersed in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution with 200 mg/L of N-CQDs were always higher, bigger and larger than those immersed in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution without N-CQDs, respectively. It could be seen from the fitting results that the Rf value reached to about 1100 ohm cm2 after adding 200 mg/L of N-CQDs. Moreover, the Rct and Qdl values of the steel plate immersed in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution with 200 mg/L of N-CQDs were much higher and lower than those immersed in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution without N-CQDs, respectively, which was consistent with the aforementioned EIS test results. These results indicated that the surface of the steel plate immersed in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution with 200 mg/L of N-CQDs was covered by an effective protective film and protected the steel plate from corrosive media [44].

The electrochemical behavior of the scratched coating in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution for 24 h was detected and shown in Fig. 6b, Fig. S13. The EIS data were fitted by ZSimpWin software using the electrical equivalent circuits in Fig. S14b. The fitting results were listed in Tables S4–S7. The Rs, Rc and Rct represented the solution, coating and charge transfer resistances, respectively [8]. The Qc and Qdl correspond to the coating constant phase element and double-layer constant phase element, respectively. A constant phase element associated with an exponent (n) was used instead of capacitor to represent the experimental deviation from the semi-circle capacitance behavior on account of the heterogeneity of the coating surface [33]. Qc is used to investigate the absorption characteristics of water in coatings and Rc is employed to measure the porosity and degradation of the coating [47]. Usually, the higher the Rc is, the more difficult it is for the corrosive media to penetrate into the coating [2]. Qdl and Rct are related to the corrosion reaction on coating/steel plate interface. The former in-situ specifies the delamination of the coating and the initiation of corrosion at the coating/steel plate interface, and the latter is related to the rate of the corrosion reaction [47]. The appearance of Warburg impedance indicated that the control step of the corrosion reaction has been changed from charge transfer step into diffusion step [48].

As evidenced from Fig. S13a, b, the capacitive loops of WEP and Gr/WEP coatings did not expand or shrink to much during the 24 h of immersion. The |Z| 0.01 Hz of the two coating also did not change too much (Fig. 6b), and the Rct values showed the same changing law (Tables S4-S5). Apparently, the capacitive loops of N-CQDs/WEP and N-CQDs@Gr/WEP coatings were obvious expanded after 3 h of immersion (Fig. S13c, d). The |Z| 0.01 Hz values also raised rapidly by an order of magnitude or so (Fig. 6b). And the Rct values of N-CQDs/WEP (2.375 × 105 ohm cm2) and N-CQDs@Gr/WEP (2.509 × 105 ohm cm2) coatings were also much higher than those of scratched WEP (4.158 × 104 ohm cm2) and Gr/WEP (3.288 × 104 ohm cm2) coatings (Tables S6-S7). After 24 h of immersion, the diameter of capacitive loops and |Z| 0.01 Hz values of N-CQDs/WEP and N-CQDs@Gr/WEP coatings were still much bigger and higher than those of scratched WEP and Gr/WEP coatings, respectively. These might be due to the fact that the N-CQDs@Fe3+ complex protective film was formed on the surface of steel plate, which prevented the corrosion process at the substrate [49] (this would be discussed in the following part).

As described in Fig. 6b, Fig. S13c, d after 3 h of immersion, the capacitive loops of N-CQDs/WEP coating gradually shrank and the |Z| 0.01 Hz, Rct values also decreased with the immersion. However, the diameter of capacitive loops, |Z| 0.01 Hz value and Rct value of N-CQDs@Gr/WEP maintained at a stable level during the 3–24 h of immersion (Fig. 6b, Tables S7). In addition, the Rc value of N-CQDs@Gr/WEP was always higher than that of N-CQDs/WEP coating during the whole immersion. This indicated that the presence of orientated graphene improved the impermeable capability of N-CQDs@Gr/WEP coating, which was conducive to slowing down the penetration of corrosive media. Under the help of orientated graphene, the damage rate of corrosive media on the N-CQDs@Fe3+ complex protective film would be slowed down, making the protection effect of N-CQDs@Fe3+ complex protective film more stable and durable. Therefore, the better corrosion resistance of scratched N-CQDs@Gr/WEP coating was attributed to synergistic effect between the orientated graphene and the N-CQDs@Fe3+ complex protective film.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of N-CQDs@Fe3+ complex protective film formation
ce:section.ce:para: The penetration of corrosive media in the scratches of coatings was quickly, so the steel plate would be slightly corroded in the initial stage of penetration. The Fe 2p XPS spectra of the steel plate underneath the damaged areas of WEP and N-CQDs/WEP coatings showed that the corroded iron mainly existed in the form of Fe3+ (Fig. S15a1, a2) [50]. The pull-off adhesion test results showed that the interface bonding forces between N-CQDs/WEP, N-CQDs@Gr/WEP coatings and the steel plates were improved due to the hydrogen bonds between the hydroxyl, carboxyl and amino groups on the surface of N-CQDs and the hydroxyl groups on the surface of steel plate (Fig. S16a, Fig. S17), demonstrating that the presence of N-CQDs at the coating/steel plate interface [51,52,53]. Moreover, EDS and EDS mapping tests were carried out on the surface of the steel plate after pulling off N-CQDs/WEP coating (in dry mode). The test results showed that the N element derived from N-CQDs was detected on the surface of the steel plate (Fig. S16b–g). This was mainly because during the process of pulling off the coating, some N-CQDs were removed with the coating, while the other parts were left on the surface of the steel plate, further indicating that the N-CQDs existed at the coating/steel plate interface and contacted with the steel plate surface (Fig. S18) (Please refer to the Supplementary material for the detailed pull-off adhesion test, EDS results and analysis).

Since N-CQDs/N-CQDs@Gr could relatively uniformly dispersed in waterborne epoxy matrix, N-CQDs/N-CQDs@Gr could be evenly distributed on the entire surface of the steel plate. Therefore, the N-CQDs@Fe3+ complexes generated by the coordination of N-CQDs and Fe3+ ions could uniformly distribute on the entire surface of the steel plate (Fig. S19a2-a3, b2-b3). As more and more N-CQDs coordinated with Fe3+ ions, more N-CQDs@Fe3+ complexes would be produced, and eventually they would connect to each other to form a stable protective film (Fig. S19a4, b4). The N element was detected on the steel plate underneath the damaged areas of N-CQDs/WEP coating by XPS, but not on the steel plate underneath the damaged areas of WEP coating (Fig. S15a3). At the same time, N element was also detected by EDS and EDS mapping on the steel plate underneath the damaged areas of N-CQDs/WEP coating (Fig. S15b1–b6). Furthermore, the iron coordination N (Fe-N, 399.2 eV) also appeared in the N 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 6c) [54]. These results all proved that N-CQDs could coordinate with Fe3+ ions and then form stable N-CQDs@Fe3+ complex protective film on the steel plate surface, protecting the steel plate from corrosive media (Fig. 7c).

The salt spray test was performed to further verify the corrosion resistance of scratched coatings (Fig. 8 ). After 13 days of testing, there was massive rust at the scratches of WEP coating, and a large number of blisters and corrosion spots were produced on the surface of WEP coating (Fig. 8a2). A large amount of rust was also generated at the scratches of Gr/WEP coating, even more than that at the scratches of WEP coating (Fig. 8b2). This might be due to the fact that the agglomerated and randomly aligned graphene accelerated the corrosion of the metal. As shown in Fig. 8c2, the vicinity of scratches of N-CQDs/WEP coating were also severely corroded, but the blisters and corrosion spots on its surface was less than that on the surface of WEP coating. This might be because the introduction of N-CQDs enhanced the cross-linking density of N-CQDs/WEP coating and formed N-CQDs@Fe3+ protective film on the surface of the steel plate, so that N-CQDs/WEP coating possessed better anti-corrosion performance than WEP coating.

However, compared with the other three coatings, the degree of corrosion at the scratches of N-CQDs@Gr/WEP coating was the lightest, exhibiting the greatest corrosion resistance (Fig. 8d2). This was consistent with the EIS results of the scratched coatings. The result could be explained by the synergistic effect of N-CQDs and orientated graphene. The former endowed the waterborne epoxy matrix the ability to form protective film on the steel plate surface. The latter prolonged the permeation paths of corrosive media to the greatest extent and restrained the corrosion-promotion activity of graphene. In addition, the surface of Gr/WEP coating was rough with small particles, which were caused by the agglomeration of graphene in waterborne epoxy matrix. However, this phenomenon was not found in N-CQDs@Gr/WEP coating, further indicating that the dispersibility and compatibility of graphene in waterborne epoxy matrix were improved after being modified by N-CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Corrosion behaviors of intact coatings in normal environment

ce:section.ce:para: EIS tests were employed to investigate the anti-corrosion performance of the coatings. Fig. 9 presented the Bode and Nyquist plots of the coatings immersed in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution for 90 days. As depicted in Fig. 9, at the initial immersion, the impedance moduli at low frequency of all coatings exhibited quickly reduction. And the -phase angle at high frequency region and the capacitive loops of all coatings also decreased and shrunk, respectively. These indicated that the corrosion media diffused into the coatings and degraded the anti-corrosion performance of coatings [48]. With the immersion time, the impedance moduli and capacitive loops of all coatings tended to be stable. However, the N-CQDs@Gr/WEP coating always exhibited the highest impedance moduli. And the -phase angle at high frequency region of N-CQDs@Gr/WEP coating decreased the least. Furthermore, the diameter of capacitive loops of the coatings had a sequence of N-CQDs@Gr/WEP > N-CQDs/WEP > Gr/WEP ≈ WEP. These results indicated that the N-CQDs@Gr/WEP coating exhibited the most outstanding anti-corrosion performance.

In order to further explore the differences in the anti-corrosion performance of the coatings during the immersion and the reasons for the differences, the |Z| 0.01 Hz values and the fb corresponding to the −45° phase angle were analyzed (Fig. 10 a, Fig. S20). The |Z| 0.01 Hz represented the corrosive media barrier ability of the coatings [48]. The fb was a criterion for evaluation of the coating delamination from the steel plate and the higher fb implied the more delaminated area at the coating/steel plate interface [55]. As evidenced from Fig. 10a, Fig. S20, the |Z| 0.01 Hz and fb of WEP and Gr/WEP coating were decreased and increased rapidly during the first 7 days of immersion, respectively, and kept at a low and high level in the subsequent tests, respectively. This manifested that the corrosion reaction occurred and the coatings began to delaminate during the 7 days of immersion. Moreover, the |Z| 0.01 Hz value of Gr/WEP coating was even lower than that of WEP coating at the initial immersion (Fig. 10a). And the fb of Gr/WEP coating was much higher than that of WEP coating throughout the immersion (Fig. S20). This could be interpreted that the defects within Gr/WEP coating caused by agglomerated graphene nanosheets were convenient for the rapid penetration of corrosive media into the coating. And the agglomerated and randomly aligned graphene nanosheets would connect with each other and the steel plate to form graphene conductive network (the electric spark leak detector issued alarm signal when carrying out electric spark leak detection test on the coating proving this, Video S1) to aggravate metal deterioration once the corrosive media penetrated [8]. The above results confirmed the inferior protective abilities of WEP and Gr/WEP coatings.

However, the degradation of N-CQDs/WEP coating was much slower than that of WEP coating. It can be clearly seen that the |Z| 0.01 Hz value of N-CQDs/WEP coating was about 1 order of magnitude higher than that of WEP coating (Fig. 10a), which was attributed to the increased cross-linking density of the coating and the formation of stable N-CQDs@Fe3+ complex protective film on the steel plate surface. Furthermore, the fb of the N-CQDs/WEP coating just increased a little during the whole immersion, which is much lower than that of WEP coating. This indicated that the formation of stable N-CQDs@Fe3+ complex film at the coating/steel plate interface was beneficial to slow down the rate of delamination (Fig. S20) [55].

In the case of N-CQDs@Gr/WEP coating, the |Z| 0.01 Hz value only slightly decreased at the initial immersion and then remained at a relatively stable value (109 ohm cm2 or so) even after 260 days of immersion, which was about 1–3 orders of magnitude higher than those of other coatings (Fig. 10a, Fig. S21). In addition, it is important to note that the N-CQDs@Gr/WEP coating had the lowest fb, which was almost zero throughout the immersion period, indicating that there was no obvious delamination at the coating/steel plate interface (Fig. S20). Some reasons could explain this excellent long-term anti-corrosion performance. First, the improved dispersibility and compatibility of graphene in waterborne epoxy matrix and the reinforced interface bonding force between graphene and waterborne epoxy matrix. Second, the orientation of graphene maximized the tortuosity of the diffusion paths for corrosive media and prevented the formation of graphene conductive network (the absence of alarm signal during the electric spark leak detection test further proved this, Video S1). Third, the formation of N-CQDs@Fe3+ complex protective film on the steel plate surface provided further protection for the steel plate.

In order to quantitative estimation of the evolution of the anti-corrosion performance of different coatings, the EIS data were further fitted by ZSimpWin software using two types of electrical equivalent circuits (Fig. S14a, b). The fitting results were listed in Tables S8–S11.

As evidenced from Fig. 10b, c and Tables S8–S11, the Qc, Qdl and Rc, Rct values of all coatings increased and reduced at the initial immersion, respectively, and tended to be stable in subsequent immersion. This was consistent with the variation of capacitive loops and |Z| 0.01 Hz. In addition, the stability of the Qc values of all the coatings after a long time of immersion meant that the coatings attained a saturate state for water (Tables S8–S11) [47]. Obviously, the N-CQDs/WEP coating exhibited much higher Rc value than that of WEP coating, proving that increasing the cross-linking density of the coating was beneficial for preventing the penetration of corrosive media. Compared with Gr/WEP and N-CQDs/WEP coatings, the Rc value of N-CQDs@Gr/WEP coating was highest and maintained at a stable level during the entire immersion. This revealed that the orientation of graphene, which maximized the barrier properties of graphene, significantly improved the barrier performance of the coating. It was noteworthy that the Qdl and Rct values of Gr/WEP coating was even higher and lower than those of WEP coating after 90 days of immersion, respectively (Fig. 10c, Tables S8-S9). This further demonstrated that the agglomerated and randomly aligned graphene accelerated charge transfer and promoted the generation of more active sites for corrosion reaction due to its superconductivity [56,57]. However, after the initial immersion, the Qdl and Rct values of N-CQDs/WEP coating almost unchanged during the long immersion, which were much lower and higher than those of WEP coating, respectively (Fig. 10c, Tables S8, S10). This behavior confirmed the hypothesis of the existence of N-CQDs@Fe3+ complex protective film on the steel plate surface, which prevented the corrosion reaction on the steel plate surface [49]. In case of N-CQDs@Gr/WEP coating, the orientation of graphene suppressed the corrosion-promotion activity of graphene, so the presence of orientated graphene did not accelerate the transfer of charge and corrosion reaction. At the same time, the presence of N-CQDs@Fe3+ complex protective film also provided further protection for the steel plate. Therefore, under the synergistic effect of the orientated graphene and the N-CQDs@Fe3+ complex protective film, the N-CQDs@Gr/WEP coating exhibited a higher and stable Qdl value and the highest Rct values during the long immersion (Fig. 10c, Tables S8–S11). These phenomena indicated that N-CQDs@Gr/WEP coating possessed the best anti-corrosion performance.

The surface morphology of the steel plates coated by different coatings after 90 days of immersion were observed by SEM. As depicted in Fig. 11 a, b, a large amount of corrosion product was observed on the surface of the steel plates coated by WEP and Gr/WEP coatings, indicating that the steel plate has been severely corroded. However, the surface of the steel plate coated by N-CQDs/WEP coating had less corrosion product compared with those coated by WEP and Gr/WEP coatings (Fig. 11a–c). Notably, dense N-CQDs@Fe3+ complex protective film was observed on the surface of the steel plate coated by N-CQDs@Gr/WEP coating (Fig. 11d). It is easily concluded that the synergistic effect of N-CQDs and orientated graphene endowed N-CQDs@Gr/WEP coating with the best corrosion resistance among the four coatings, which was consistent with EIS results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Corrosion behaviors of intact coatings in harsh oxygen corrosive environment
Anti-corrosion performance of coatings in harsh oxygen corrosive environment
ce:section.ce:para: EIS tests were carried out again to assess the anti-corrosion performance of the coatings in 3 MPa pure O2 and 3.5 wt% NaCl solution coupling environment (Fig. 12 a, Fig. S22). After 10 days of immersion, the |Z| 0.01 Hz values of WEP and Gr/WEP coatings reduced to 5.3 × 107 ohm cm2 and 3.4 × 107 ohm cm2, respectively (Fig. 12a). Additionally, a large amount of corrosion product was generated on the surface of the steel plates coated by the WEP and Gr/WEP coatings, and some areas of the two coatings were separated from the steel plates (Fig. 12d1, d2, Fig. S23a, b), proving that the steel plates were severely corroded.

On the contrary, the surface of the steel plates coated by N-CQDs/WEP and N-CQDs@Gr/WEP coatings were covered by dense protective films (Fig. 12d3, d4), and no corrosion was observed on the surface of the two coatings (Fig. S23c, d). Unexpectedly, the |Z| 0.01 Hz values of N-CQDs/WEP and N-CQDs@Gr/WEP coatings were both above 1010 ohm cm2 after 10 days of immersion in this 3 MPa pure O2 and 3.5 wt% NaCl solution coupling environment (Fig. 12a), which were higher than those obtained after 10 days of immersion in the normal environment with only 3.5 wt% NaCl solution (Fig. S24). Thus, we inferred that the protective film induced by N-CQDs in this harsh oxygen corrosive environment might be different from that in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution environment. The further detection and exploration were carried out.

XPS was used to detect the composition of the dense protective film. Fig. 12b, c showed the Fe 2p XPS spectra of the steel plate surfaces underneath WEP and N-CQDs/WEP coatings after 10 days of immersion in harsh oxygen corrosive environment, respectively. In Fig. 12b, two obvious peaks located at 711.0 eV and 724.6 eV were observed, and could be divided into peaks at 710.6 eV, 724.2 eV and 712.0 eV, 725.6 eV, which were characteristic peaks of Fe3+ in Fe2O3 and FeOOH, respectively [50,58]. This also indicated the steel plate was severe corroded. However, the peaks of Fe 2p in Fig. 12c were shifted to lower binding energy (710.3 eV and 724.0 eV), which was not obvious in the specimens tested in normal environment with only 3.5 wt% NaCl solution (Fig. S15a1, a2). And the two peaks could be separated into peaks at 710.0 and 723.6 eV for Fe2+ and 711.4 and 725.0 eV for Fe3+, respectively [50,59], which were characteristic peaks of Fe3O4. In addition, the satellite peak of γ- Fe2O3 at 718.8 eV was also observed (Fig. 12c) [50]. These results showed that the main components of the protective film were γ-Fe2O3 and Fe3O4, proving that the protective film induced by N-CQDs in 3 MPa pure O2 and 3.5 wt% NaCl solution coupling environment was mainly passive film. This was different from the N-CQDs@ Fe3+ complex film formed in the normal environment with only 3.5 wt% NaCl solution.

Previous study proved that N-CQDs possessed profuse edge sites and greater specific surface area, which were favorable for the fast electron transfer [60]. And the doping of N atoms could make the graphite materials lose electroneutrality to create charged sites that was favorable for O2 adsorption, which seemed to be a key factor for weaking the OO bonds and enhancing oxygen reduction reaction activity [21,22]. The pull-off adhesion test results showed that the introduction of N-CQDs enhanced the interface bonding forces between N-CQDs/WEP, N-CQDs@Gr/WEP coatings and the steel plates due to the hydrogen bonds between the hydroxyl, carboxyl and amino groups on the surface of N-CQDs and the hydroxyl groups on the surface of steel plate (Fig. S16a, Fig. S17) [51,52,53]. And the EDS and EDS mapping also detected N element derived from on the surface of steel plate after pulling off N-CQDs/WEP coating (in dry mode) (Fig. S16b–g, please refer to the Supplementary material for the detailed pull-off adhesion test, EDS results and analysis). These indicated the presence of N-CQDs at the coating/steel plate interface.

When the permeated oxygen molecules reached the coating/steel plate surface, they would be adsorbed on the active sites of N-CQDs, and the OO bonds would be broken to generate oxygen atoms (O2(g) → *O2 → *O, asterisks denote active surface sites) (Fig. 13 a, b) [21,60]. The oxygen atoms had a high reactivity with iron, and the two could react to form dense oxides [48,61,62]. Since N-CQDs/N-CQDs@Gr could relatively uniformly dispersed in waterborne epoxy matrix, N-CQDs/N-CQDs@Gr could be relatively uniformly distributed on the entire surface of steel plate. Therefore, the dense oxides could spread all over the surface of steel plate. In 3 MPa pure O2 and 3.5 wt% NaCl solution coupling environment, the rate of oxygen penetration into the coating would be accelerated, which was much faster than that in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution environment. Therefore, a large amount of oxygen could be transferred to the coating/steel plate interface in a short period of time to produce enough of such dense oxides, then a dense oxide film would be rapidly formed covering the whole surface of the steel plate and providing protection for the steel plate (Fig. 13b).

To conclude, the dense passive film, which was observed via SEM and confirmed via XPS, was proposed to have been formed by atomic oxygen at the active sites where N-CQDs contacted with the steel plate and protected the steel plate well. As for the reason why mainly dense passive film rather than N-CQDs@ Fe3+ complex film was formed in the high-pressure oxygen corrosive environment, it might be because that the mass transfer rate and quantity of oxygen were significantly enhanced, resulting in the greatly increased generation rate of passive film, so that the passive film was generated preferring to complex protective film.

The schematic diagrams for the protective mechanisms of the coatings were shown in Fig. 14 . For the WEP coating (Fig. 14a), the corrosion media could quickly reach the surface of the steel plate and caused severe corrosion. After adding graphene into the waterborne epoxy matrix, the graphene agglomerated seriously and caused many defects. Moreover, the agglomerated and randomly aligned graphene could form an electrochemical circuit with the steel plate (graphene serves as cathode, and metal acts as anode) to accelerate the corrosion of metal once the corrosive media permeate (Fig. 14b).

In term of N-CQDs/WEP coating, the protective mechanism could be divided into two parts (Fig. 14c): (1) N-CQDs increase the cross-linking density of the coating, (2) the stable protective film formed at coating/steel plate interface covered the whole surface of the steel plate and reduced the corrosion rate of the steel plate. Notably, the protective film formed in the normal environment with only 3.5 wt% NaCl solution was mainly N-CQDs@Fe3+ complex protective film, whereas in high-pressure oxygen and 3.5 wt% NaCl solution coupling environment was mainly dense passive film.

However, only employing N-CQDs as filler could not endow waterborne epoxy coating with sufficiently excellent anti-corrosion performance to resist long-term or harsh oxygen corrosion. In the case of N-CQDs@Gr/WEP coating, the modification of N-CQDs improved the dispersibility and compatibility of graphene in waterborne epoxy matrix, so that the graphene could be aligned parallel to the steel plate surface under high centrifugal and high shear forces. The N-CQDs@Gr/WEP coating not only possessed the advantages of the aforementioned N-CQDs/WEP coating, but also most important of all, the orientation of the graphene restrained the formation of graphene conductive network and exerted the barrier effect of graphene to the greatest degree (Fig. 14d). The synergistic effect of N-CQDs and oriented graphene significantly enhanced the anti-corrosion performance of waterborne epoxy coating.

In this study, we used a low-cost and eco-friendly strategy to creatively prepared long-term dispersion-stabilized graphene aqueous dispersion using N-CQDs as dispersant without destroying the original structure of graphene. At the same time, the modified graphene nanosheets were successfully orientated in horizontal direction in waterborne epoxy matrix via a facile spin coating method. It is worth noting that no organic solvent was used throughout the preparation process of the filler and coatings. The N-CQDs@Gr/WEP coating still exhibited excellent anti-corrosion performance in spite of being immersed in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution for 260 days. Interestingly, after 10 days of immersion in harsh oxygen corrosive environment, the |Z| 0.01 Hz value of N-CQDs@Gr/WEP coating still remained at 1010 ohm cm2 or so, which was approximately three orders of magnitude higher than that of WEP coating. The protection mechanism for N-CQDs@Gr/WEP coating was originated from the triple promotion effects of the reinforced interfacial interaction, the protective film on steel plate surface and the orientation of graphene. In summary, the water-redispersible graphene powder would greatly facilitate the transportation of graphene. And the extremely simple physical method to resolve the contradiction on the anti-corrosion capacity of graphene would give inspiration to the fabrication of graphene waterborne coatings with improved anti-corrosion performance. We believe that this high-efficiency anti-corrosion strategy will have promising application prospects in not only harsh oxygen corrosive environments, but also other areas such as deep ocean and gases transportation.
